🐍 How to use this book 
Every topic in this book follows the same gentle recipe so you always know what's coming next: 
🐍 What is it? — a real-life story or analogy, no jargon. 
🐍 Why do we need it? — what real problems it solves. 
🐍 Python note — any Python feature you need for this topic. 
🐍 Code — heavily commented, built from scratch. 
🐍 Dry run — a step-by-step trace of variables changing. 
🐍 Complexity — in plain English first, then Big-O. 
🐍 Interview Questions — the classics, with solutions. 
🐍 Quiz — small puzzles to test your understanding. 
Don't rush. Re-read the dry runs. Type the code yourself — muscle memory matters. If a concept 
feels fuzzy, the analogy at the top usually clears it up. Ready? Let's go.

DSA with Python 
— Page 4 — 
🐍 Section 0 — Python Crash Course for DSA 
🐍 Why this section exists 
Before we learn Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA), we need a friend who can actually talk to 
the computer for us. That friend is Python. Don't worry — we only need a small corner of Python 
to solve 99% of DSA problems. Think of this section as learning the 26 letters before writing a 
novel. 
0.1 What is Python? 
Python is a programming language — a way of writing instructions for your computer using words that 
look almost like English. If you can read a grocery list, you can read Python. 
# This is a comment. The computer ignores it. 
print("Hello, DSA!") # This prints text on the screen. 
🐍 Why Python for DSA? It is short, readable, and has built-in tools (lists, dicts, sets, heaps) that save 
us hours of work. In interviews, writing 10 clean Python lines beats writing 40 messy C++ lines. 
0.2 Variables and Data Types 
🐍 What is a variable? A variable is a labelled box where you store a value. If I write x = 5, I'm saying 
'Hey Python, make a box called x and put 5 inside it.' Later I can say x + 3 and Python knows it means 5 + 
3 = 8. 
# Numbers 
age = 10 # int — whole number 
height = 4.5 # float — number with a decimal 
# Text 
name = "Alex" # str — a string of characters 
# Yes / No 
is_happy = True # bool — True or False (capital T/F!) 
# Nothing 
result = None # NoneType — means "no value yet" 
✅ Dynamic typing 
You never say what type a variable is — Python figures it out from the value. You can even change 
the type later: x = 5 then x = "hi" is legal.

DSA with Python 
— Page 5 — 
0.3 Input and Output 
# Print something to the screen 
print("Your score is", 95) # Output: Your score is 95 
 
# Read something from the user (always returns a string!) 
name = input("Your name: ") # user types: Alex → name becomes "Alex" 
num = int(input("A num: ")) # wrap with int(...) to get a number 
print(f"Hi {name}, double is {num*2}") # f-string = easy templating 
0.4 Conditionals (if / elif / else) 
🐍 Analogy: A conditional is a fork in the road. 'IF it is raining, take an umbrella; ELSE wear sunglasses.' 
The computer picks exactly one path. 
marks = 72 
if marks >= 90: 
 grade = "A" # runs if marks is 90 or more 
elif marks >= 75: 
 grade = "B" # runs if not A but >= 75 
elif marks >= 50: 
 grade = "C" 
else: 
 grade = "Fail" # everything else 
print(grade) # Output: C 
🐍 Indentation matters! 
Python uses 4 spaces (or a tab) to mean 'this line belongs inside the if.' Other languages use { } — 
Python uses whitespace. Mess this up and the code breaks. 
0.5 Loops (for and while) 
🐍 Analogy: A loop is a 'repeat-this-chore' command. 'For every plate on the table, wash it.' That's a for 
loop. 'While there is dirty water in the sink, drain some.' That's a while loop. 
# for loop — runs once per item in a collection 
for i in range(5): # i = 0,1,2,3,4 
 print(i) 
 
# range(start, stop, step) 
for i in range(2, 11, 2): # 2,4,6,8,10 
 print(i)

DSA with Python 
— Page 6 — 
# while loop — runs while the condition is True 
n = 5 
while n > 0: 
 print(n) 
 n = n - 1 # very important: change n, else infinite loop! 
✅ range() is your best friend in DSA 
range(n) gives 0..n-1, range(a,b) gives a..b-1, range(a,b,-1) counts DOWN. We use it everywhere in 
loops. 
0.6 Functions 
🐍 Analogy: A function is like a kitchen appliance. A blender doesn't care WHAT fruit you put in; it just 
blends. You press the button (call it), give it input (ingredients), and get output (smoothie). 
def add(a, b): # 'def' defines a function named add 
 return a + b # 'return' sends the answer back out 
 
result = add(3, 7) # call the function with 3 and 7 
print(result) # Output: 10 
 
def greet(name="friend"): # default parameter 
 return f"Hi, {name}!" 
print(greet()) # Hi, friend! 
print(greet("Alex")) # Hi, Alex! 
0.7 Recursion (in one paragraph for now) 
🐍 Analogy: A function that calls itself. Think of Russian nesting dolls — to describe a doll you say 'a 
bigger doll containing THIS (a smaller doll).' We cover recursion fully in Section 4; for now, just know this 
word exists. 
def factorial(n): 
 if n == 0: # base case — stops the recursion 
 return 1 
 return n * factorial(n - 1) # calls itself with smaller n 
print(factorial(5)) # 120 
0.8 Lists — the most important DSA tool 
🐍 Analogy: A list is a row of numbered boxes in a single train. Each box holds one value. Indexes start 
at 0 (not 1 — blame the engineers).

DSA with Python 
— Page 7 — 
nums = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] 
# 0 1 2 3 4 ← index 
print(nums[0]) # 10 — first element 
print(nums[-1]) # 50 — last element (negative = from the end) 
print(nums[1:4]) # [20, 30, 40] — slicing (from 1 up to but NOT 4) 
 
nums.append(60) # add at end → [10,20,30,40,50,60] 
nums.insert(0, 5) # insert 5 at index 0 → [5,10,20,30,40,50,60] 
nums.pop() # remove last → returns 60 
nums.remove(20) # remove first 20 
len(nums) # length 
20 in nums # True / False membership test 
nums.sort() # sort in place (ascending) 
nums.reverse() # reverse in place 
sum(nums) # total 
max(nums), min(nums) 
🐍 Time complexity of list operations (memorise this!) 
append(x), pop() (from end) → O(1) — instant 
pop(0), insert(0, x) → O(n) — shifts everything 
x in list → O(n) — scans all items 
list[i] → O(1) — direct access 
list.sort() → O(n log n) 
0.9 Tuples 
A tuple is a list that cannot be changed (immutable). Good for fixed data like (latitude, longitude) or 
returning multiple values from a function. 
point = (3, 4) 
x, y = point # unpack — x=3, y=4 
# point[0] = 99 ← ERROR! tuples can't be modified 
0.10 Sets 
🐍 Analogy: A set is a bag of UNIQUE items. Like a box of unique stamps — you can't have two copies 
of the same stamp. No order, no duplicates, but super fast membership checks. 
s = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1} # duplicates get auto-removed → {1,2,3} 
s.add(4) # {1,2,3,4} 
s.remove(2) 
3 in s # True, and this is O(1)!

DSA with Python 
— Page 8 — 
s1 = {1,2,3}; s2 = {2,3,4} 
s1 | s2 # union → {1,2,3,4} 
s1 & s2 # intersection→ {2,3} 
s1 - s2 # difference → {1} 
✅ Why sets are magical in DSA 
Checking 'have I seen this value before?' takes O(1) with a set vs O(n) with a list. For problems like 
'find duplicates,' sets turn O(n²) into O(n). 
0.11 Dictionaries 
🐍 Analogy: A dictionary stores key → value pairs, like a phone book: name → phone number. Lookup 
by key is instant (O(1)). 
marks = {"Alex": 85, "Riya": 92, "Sam": 76} 
print(marks["Alex"]) # 85 
marks["Zoe"] = 88 # add new 
marks["Alex"] = 90 # update 
del marks["Sam"] # delete 
"Alex" in marks # True (checks keys, O(1)) 
for k, v in marks.items(): # loop through 
 print(k, v) 
marks.get("Mia", 0) # returns 0 if "Mia" missing (no crash) 
0.12 List Comprehensions — the Python superpower 
A compact way to build a list in one line. Reads as 'make this, for each thing in that, if condition'. 
# squares of 0..9 
squares = [i*i for i in range(10)] 
 
# even numbers from a list 
nums = [1,2,3,4,5,6] 
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0] # [2,4,6] 
 
# 2D grid of zeros — 3 rows, 4 cols 
grid = [[0]*4 for _ in range(3)] 
🐍 Common 2D list bug 
Never write grid = [[0]*4]*3 — that creates 3 references to the SAME row, so changing one 
changes all three. Always use the list comprehension form above.

DSA with Python 
— Page 9 — 
0.13 The collections module 
from collections import deque, defaultdict, Counter, OrderedDict 
 
# deque — double-ended queue, O(1) at BOTH ends (list.pop(0) is O(n)!) 
q = deque([1,2,3]) 
q.append(4) # add right 
q.appendleft(0) # add left 
q.pop() # remove right 
q.popleft() # remove left ← this is the magic one 
 
# defaultdict — dict with a default value for missing keys 
dd = defaultdict(int) # missing key → 0 
dd["a"] += 1 # no KeyError, starts at 0 
dd2 = defaultdict(list) # missing key → [] 
dd2["friends"].append("Sam") 
 
# Counter — counts frequencies in one line 
Counter("mississippi") # {'i':4,'s':4,'p':2,'m':1} 
Counter([1,2,2,3,3,3]) # {3:3,2:2,1:1} 
0.14 heapq — heaps / priority queues 
A heap is a tree where the smallest (or largest) item is always on top. heapq gives us min-heap 
operations in O(log n). 
import heapq 
h = [] 
heapq.heappush(h, 5) 
heapq.heappush(h, 1) 
heapq.heappush(h, 3) 
heapq.heappop(h) # returns 1 (smallest) — O(log n) 
# For a max-heap, push negatives: heappush(h, -x), pop → -heappop(h) 
0.15 sys.setrecursionlimit 
Python caps recursion depth at ~1000 by default. Deep DSA problems (like recursion on 10^5 nodes) 
need this line at the top: 
import sys 
sys.setrecursionlimit(10**6) # allow up to 1 million recursive calls

🐍 You now know enough Python for all of DSA. 
Seriously. Every problem in this book uses only what you just learned. Let's go build real data 
structures.

DSA with Python 
— Page 11 — 
🐍 Section 1 — Arrays & Strings 
1.1 What is an Array? 
📖 What is it? 
Imagine a row of mailboxes bolted to a wall, numbered 0, 1, 2, 3… Each mailbox holds exactly one letter. 
An array is exactly that: a row of memory slots, each holding one value, each reachable instantly by its 
number (the index). 
🐍 Mailbox analogy 
To find mailbox #7, the postman doesn't walk past boxes 0,1,2,3,4,5,6 — he walks straight to box 7 
because they're the same distance apart. That's why array[i] is O(1): a single multiplication, one 
jump. 
📖 Why do we need it? 
Arrays are the most basic way to keep a list of things together — scores, temperatures, pixels, anything. 
Almost every other data structure (stacks, queues, heaps, hash tables) is built ON TOP of an array inside. 
📖 Python note 
Python's list IS its array. It's a dynamic array — it grows when full. So when we say 'array' in this book, 
we mean a Python list. 
📖 Code: basic operations 
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] 
 
# 1. Access — O(1) 
print(arr[2]) # 30 
 
# 2. Traverse — O(n) 
for i, v in enumerate(arr): 
 print(i, v) 
 
# 3. Search (linear) — O(n) 
target = 30 
found = -1 
for i in range(len(arr)): 
 if arr[i] == target: 
 found = i 
 break 
print("found at", found) # found at 2

DSA with Python 
— Page 12 — 
 
# 4. Insert at end — O(1) 
arr.append(60) 
 
# 5. Insert at position 2 — O(n) 
arr.insert(2, 25) # [10,20,25,30,40,50,60] 
 
# 6. Delete value — O(n) 
arr.remove(25) # removes first 25 
 
# 7. Delete by index — O(n) if not end, O(1) if end 
arr.pop() # removes 60, O(1) 
arr.pop(0) # removes 10, O(n)

📖 Time & Space 
🐍 In plain English first 
Access by index: one jump — instant. 
Search for a value: in the worst case you look at every box → 'grows linearly with n'. 
Insert/delete in the middle: you must shift everyone — also linear. 
Space used: one slot per item → linear (O(n)). 
1.2 2D Arrays (grids / matrices) 
A 2D array is an array of arrays — rows and columns, like a chessboard, a spreadsheet, or pixels of an 
image. 
# 3 rows × 4 columns filled with 0 
grid = [[0]*4 for _ in range(3)] 
 
grid[1][2] = 7 # row 1, column 2

DSA with Python 
— Page 13 — 
# Traverse every cell 
for r in range(len(grid)): 
 for c in range(len(grid[0])): 
 print(grid[r][c], end=" ") 
 print() 
✅ Rows vs cols 
rows = len(grid), cols = len(grid[0]). grid[r][c] means 'row r, column c'. Mixing them up is the #1 grid 
bug — draw it on paper. 
1.3 The Sliding Window Technique 
📖 What is it? 
Imagine looking at a long train through a small window on a moving car. You can see exactly 3 
compartments at once. As the car moves, one compartment slides in on the right, one slides out on the 
left. That's a sliding window over an array. 
📖 Why? 
Problems like 'max sum of 5 consecutive numbers' or 'longest substring without repeat characters' 
become O(n) instead of O(n·k) if we just slide a window and update the running answer, instead of re-
computing from scratch every step. 
📖 Example: max sum of any k consecutive elements 
def max_sum_window(arr, k): 
 # Step 1: sum of first window 
 window = sum(arr[:k]) 
 best = window 
 # Step 2: slide from index k to end 
 for i in range(k, len(arr)): 
 window += arr[i] # add the new right element 
 window -= arr[i - k] # remove the old left element 
 best = max(best, window) 
 return best 
 
print(max_sum_window([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], 3)) # 9 (5+1+3)

1.4 Two Pointer Technique 
📖 What is it? 
Use two index variables — often one at the start and one at the end — that walk towards each other (or 
together) instead of using two nested loops. It takes O(n²) brute force down to O(n). 
📖 Example: pair sum in a sorted array 
def has_pair(arr, target): 
 left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1 
 while left < right: 
 s = arr[left] + arr[right] 
 if s == target: 
 return True 
 elif s < target: # need bigger → move left pointer right 
 left += 1 
 else: # need smaller → move right pointer left 
 right -= 1 
 return False 
 
print(has_pair([1,2,4,6,8,11], 10)) # True (2+8)

1.5 Prefix Sum & Difference Arrays 
📖 What is it?

DSA with Python 
— Page 15 — 
A prefix sum array stores running totals. If prefix[i] is the sum of arr[0..i-1], then the sum of any range 
[L..R] is simply prefix[R+1] - prefix[L] — in O(1)! 
def build_prefix(arr): 
 pre = [0] * (len(arr) + 1) 
 for i in range(len(arr)): 
 pre[i+1] = pre[i] + arr[i] 
 return pre 
 
arr = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6] 
pre = build_prefix(arr) 
# sum of arr[2..5] = pre[6] - pre[2] = 19 - 4 = 15 
print(pre[6] - pre[2]) # 15 
🐍 Why this matters 
Without prefix sums, answering 1,000,000 range-sum queries takes 10¹² operations. With prefix 
sums, it's 10⁶. That's 1,000,000× faster. 
1.6 Kadane's Algorithm — Maximum Subarray Sum 
📖 The problem 
Given an array with positive AND negative numbers, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum. 
📖 The insight 
At each index, ask yourself: 'Should I extend the current streak, or should I start fresh here?' Take 
whichever gives a bigger running sum. 
def max_subarray(arr): 
 best = cur = arr[0] 
 for x in arr[1:]: 
 cur = max(x, cur + x) # extend or restart 
 best = max(best, cur) # remember the best ever seen 
 return best 
 
print(max_subarray([-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4])) # 6 → [4,-1,2,1]

1.7 Strings — arrays of characters 
A string is just an array of characters under the hood. Most array techniques apply directly. Key thing: in 
Python, strings are IMMUTABLE — you cannot do s[0] = 'H'. You must build a new string. 
s = "hello" 
print(s[0]) # 'h' 
print(s[-1]) # 'o' 
print(s[1:4]) # 'ell' 
print(s[::-1]) # 'olleh' — reverse! 
print(len(s)) # 5 
print(s.upper()) # 'HELLO' 
print("l" in s) # True 
# Build a modified string: 
chars = list(s) 
chars[0] = 'H' 
s2 = "".join(chars) # 'Hello' 
Palindrome check 
def is_palindrome(s): 
 # Lowercase and strip non-letters 
 clean = "".join(c.lower() for c in s if c.isalnum()) 
 return clean == clean[::-1] # compare to its reverse 
 
print(is_palindrome("A man, a plan, a canal: Panama")) # True 
Anagram check

DSA with Python 
— Page 17 — 
def are_anagrams(a, b): 
 return sorted(a) == sorted(b) # O(n log n) 
 
# Faster: using Counter — O(n) 
from collections import Counter 
def are_anagrams_fast(a, b): 
 return Counter(a) == Counter(b) 
 
print(are_anagrams("listen", "silent")) # True 
🐍 Most Asked Interview Questions — Arrays & Strings 
Q1. Two Sum 
Given an array and a target, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to the target. 
def two_sum(nums, target): 
 seen = {} # value → index 
 for i, x in enumerate(nums): 
 need = target - x 
 if need in seen: # O(1) lookup 
 return [seen[need], i] 
 seen[x] = i 
 return [] 
 
print(two_sum([2,7,11,15], 9)) # [0,1] 
Idea: as we walk through the array, we ask the dictionary 'have I already seen the partner I need?' This 
turns O(n²) brute force into O(n). 
Q2. Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock 
One transaction only — maximize profit. 
def max_profit(prices): 
 lo = float('inf') # cheapest price seen so far 
 best = 0 
 for p in prices: 
 lo = min(lo, p) # remember the cheapest 
 best = max(best, p - lo) # profit if we sell today 
 return best 
 
print(max_profit([7,1,5,3,6,4])) # 5 (buy at 1, sell at 6) 
Q3. Maximum Subarray 
See Kadane's algorithm above. This is asked literally everywhere.

DSA with Python 
— Page 18 — 
Q4. Rotate Array by k steps 
def rotate(nums, k): 
 n = len(nums) 
 k %= n # handle k > n 
 nums[:] = nums[-k:] + nums[:-k] # slick one-liner 
 
# Alternative: 3 reversals, O(1) extra space 
def rotate_inplace(nums, k): 
 n = len(nums); k %= n 
 nums.reverse() 
 nums[:k] = reversed(nums[:k]) 
 nums[k:] = reversed(nums[k:]) 
Q5. Trapping Rain Water 
For each bar, water trapped = min(max-left, max-right) - bar. We pre-compute both with one pass each 
(O(n) time, O(n) space). 
def trap(height): 
 n = len(height) 
 if n < 3: return 0 
 left_max = [0]*n 
 right_max = [0]*n 
 left_max[0] = height[0] 
 for i in range(1, n): 
 left_max[i] = max(left_max[i-1], height[i]) 
 right_max[-1] = height[-1] 
 for i in range(n-2, -1, -1): 
 right_max[i] = max(right_max[i+1], height[i]) 
 water = 0 
 for i in range(n): 
 water += min(left_max[i], right_max[i]) - height[i] 
 return water 
 
print(trap([0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1])) # 6 
🐍 Tricky Quiz — Arrays & Strings 
🐍 Q1. Output puzzle 
a = [1,2,3] 
b = a 
b.append(4) 
print(a)

DSA with Python 
— Page 19 — 
 
Answer: [1,2,3,4] — b and a point to the SAME list in memory. Assignment doesn't copy. 
🐍 Q2. MCQ — which is O(1)? 
A) list.insert(0, x) B) list.append(x) C) list.remove(x) D) 'x' in list 
 
Answer: B. Append adds at the end — no shifting. All others are O(n). 
🐍 Q3. Output 
grid = [[0]*3]*3 
grid[0][0] = 1 
print(grid) 
 
Answer: [[1,0,0],[1,0,0],[1,0,0]]. All 3 rows share the SAME inner list — classic bug. Use [[0]*3 for _ 
in range(3)] instead.

DSA with Python 
— Page 20 — 
🐍 Section 2 — Searching Algorithms 
2.1 Linear Search 
📖 What is it? 
Look at every element, one by one, until you find what you want. Like searching for your friend's name 
in an unsorted list by reading top to bottom. 
def linear_search(arr, target): 
 for i, x in enumerate(arr): 
 if x == target: 
 return i 
 return -1 
Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Works on ANY array, sorted or not. 
2.2 Binary Search 
📖 The magic trick 
Imagine a dictionary. To find 'Python,' you don't start from 'A.' You open the middle: maybe you land on 
'M.' 'Python' is after M, so you throw away the first half. Now you look at the middle of the remaining 
half. Throw away half again. And again. In 20 steps you can find ANY word in a million-word dictionary. 
That's binary search. 
🐍 Precondition! 
Binary search ONLY works on sorted arrays. If the array is unsorted, sort it first — or use linear 
search. 
Iterative version 
def binary_search(arr, target): 
 lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1 
 while lo <= hi:

3.5 Quick Sort 
📖 The pivot party 
Pick one number (the 'pivot'). Push everything smaller to its left, everything bigger to its right. Now the 
pivot is in its final sorted position. Recursively sort the left and right groups. On average: O(n log n). 
Worst case (bad pivot every time): O(n²). 
def quick_sort(arr, lo=0, hi=None): 
 if hi is None: hi = len(arr) - 1 
 if lo < hi:

 quick_sort(arr, p + 1, hi) 
 
def partition(arr, lo, hi): 
 pivot = arr[hi] # choose last as pivot 
 i = lo - 1 # boundary of 'smaller' zone 
 for j in range(lo, hi): 
 if arr[j] <= pivot: 
 i += 1 
 arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i] 
 arr[i+1], arr[hi] = arr[hi], arr[i+1] # put pivot in place 
 return i + 1

3.6 Counting Sort, Radix Sort, Bucket Sort (non-comparison) 
Comparison-based sorts can't beat O(n log n). But if the input has a restricted shape — e.g. 'all integers 
from 0 to 100' — we can cheat and sort in O(n). 
Counting Sort 
def counting_sort(arr): 
 if not arr: return arr 
 mx = max(arr) 
 count = [0] * (mx + 1) 
 for x in arr: count[x] += 1 # tally 
 out = [] 
 for v, c in enumerate(count): # rebuild 
 out.extend([v] * c) 
 return out 
Time: O(n + k) where k = range of values. Space: O(k). Only works on small-range integers. 
Radix Sort extends counting sort digit by digit (LSD to MSD) for larger numbers. Bucket Sort drops 
elements into n buckets by value range, then sorts each small bucket. Both useful when input is well-
distributed.

🐍 Section 4 — Recursion & Backtracking 
4.1 What is Recursion? 
🐍 Russian dolls 
Open a matryoshka doll: inside is a smaller doll. Open that: an even smaller doll. Eventually you hit 
the smallest solid doll — you can't open it. That's recursion: a problem defined in terms of a 
smaller version of itself, stopping at a smallest case we solve directly. 
📖 Definition 
A function that calls itself. Every recursive function has two parts: a BASE CASE (the smallest problem 
you just answer directly) and a RECURSIVE CASE (solve a smaller version, then combine). 
🐍 Without a base case… 
…you get infinite recursion → stack overflow → RecursionError. Always write the base case FIRST, 
then the recursive part. 
4.2 Factorial — the classic first example 
def factorial(n): 
 # Base case 
 if n <= 1: 
 return 1 
 # Recursive case 
 return n * factorial(n - 1) 
📖 The call stack for factorial(4) 
factorial(4) 
 → 4 * factorial(3) 
 → 3 * factorial(2) 
 → 2 * factorial(1) 
 → 1 ← base case 
 → 2 * 1 = 2 
 → 3 * 2 = 6 
 → 4 * 6 = 24 
Every recursive call is pushed on the call stack. When we hit the base case, calls unwind, each returning 
its result upward. The stack uses O(n) memory.

4.4 Base case vs Recursive case — the 3-step recipe 
1. Identify the BASE CASE — the smallest input you can answer without thinking. 
2. Write the RECURSIVE CASE — assume the function works on a smaller input, use its result. 
3. Verify it gets smaller each call — otherwise infinite loop.

def permutations(nums): 
 result = [] 
 def backtrack(path, used): 
 if len(path) == len(nums): 
 result.append(path[:]) # copy the current path 
 return 
 for i in range(len(nums)): 
 if used[i]: continue 
 used[i] = True 
 path.append(nums[i]) 
 backtrack(path, used) # recurse 
 path.pop() # undo 
 used[i] = False 
 backtrack([], [False]*len(nums)) 
 return result

5.1 What is a Linked List? 
🐍 Train compartments 
Picture a train: each compartment holds passengers (data) AND a connecting rod to the NEXT 
compartment. Unlike a row of mailboxes (array), the compartments don't need to sit together in 
memory — they can be scattered anywhere, linked only by those rods ('pointers'). 
A node has two things: (1) a value, and (2) a reference to the next node. The list is the first node — from 
there you can walk the whole chain.

from collections import OrderedDict 
class LRUCache: 
 def __init__(self, capacity): 
 self.cap = capacity 
 self.cache = OrderedDict() 
 
 def get(self, key): 
 if key not in self.cache: return -1 
 self.cache.move_to_end(key) # mark as most recent 
 return self.cache[key] 
 
 def put(self, key, value): 
 if key in self.cache: 
 self.cache.move_to_end(key) 
 self.cache[key] = value 
 if len(self.cache) > self.cap: 
 self.cache.popitem(last=False) # drop least recent

7.1 What is a Hash Table? 
🐍 Lockers at the gym 
You walk into a gym with 1000 lockers. Instead of checking every one to find yours, the front desk 
applies a simple rule: 'Member ID 1234? → locker number (1234 mod 100) = locker 34.' That rule is 
a HASH FUNCTION. Given a key, it tells you instantly where to look. That's a hash table in one 
picture. 
A hash table (Python's dict/set) uses a hash function to turn a key into an array index. On average, 
lookup / insert / delete are O(1) — constant time, no matter how many items you have. 
7.2 How Python's dict and set work 
Python dicts use an open-addressed hash table. When two keys hit the same slot (a COLLISION), Python 
probes forward for the next empty slot. Behind the scenes this is fast and amortised O(1). 
🐍 Important rule 
Only HASHABLE objects can be dict keys or set elements. Immutable things are hashable: int, str, 
tuple of hashables. Lists, dicts, sets are NOT hashable (they can be changed, which would break the 
hash). 
7.3 Collision handling (concept only) 
• 
CHAINING — each slot holds a linked list of colliding items. Used by Java HashMap. 
• 
OPEN ADDRESSING — on collision, probe to the next slot. Used by Python. 
• 
Keep load factor < ~70% — resize (rehash) when too full.

8.4 Binary Search Tree (BST) 
🐍 The BST rule 
For every node: everything in the LEFT subtree is LESS, everything in the RIGHT subtree is 
GREATER. This turns a tree into a searchable structure — O(log n) if balanced! 
def bst_search(root, target): 
 cur = root

 if target == cur.val: return cur 
 cur = cur.left if target < cur.val else cur.right 
 return None

 if root is None: return TreeNode(val)

 root.left = bst_insert(root.left, val)

 root.right = bst_insert(root.right, val)

O(n) search. To stay O(log n), use a self-balancing BST like AVL or Red-Black.

9.1 What is a Heap? 
🐍 Tournament bracket 
In a knockout tournament, the champion ends up at the top. The semi-finalists are just below. 
That's a heap: a binary tree where the TOP is always the minimum (min-heap) or maximum (max-
heap) of everything inside. 
A heap is a COMPLETE binary tree (filled level by level, left to right) with the HEAP PROPERTY: parent ≤ 
children (min-heap) or parent ≥ children (max-heap). 
9.2 Array representation of a heap 
Because a heap is complete, we can store it in a plain array. For index i: 
• 
Parent = (i − 1) // 2 
• 
Left = 2·i + 1 
• 
Right = 2·i + 2 
No pointers, no nodes — just clever indexing.

9.5 How heappush / heappop work (sift-up and sift-down) 
Push: put new item at the end, then 'sift up' — compare with parent, swap if out of order, repeat. Log n 
levels → O(log n). 
Pop: take the root (answer), move the last element to the root, then 'sift down' — swap with the smaller 
child, repeat. Also O(log n).

10.8 Dijkstra's Algorithm (shortest path, non-negative weights) 
📖 Idea 
A 'greedy' BFS with a priority queue instead of a regular queue. Always expand the UNVISITED node with 
the currently smallest known distance. Once popped, that node's distance is final. 
import heapq 
def dijkstra(n, adj, src): 
 dist = [float('inf')] * n 
 dist[src] = 0 
 h = [(0, src)] 
 while h: 
 d, u = heapq.heappop(h) 
 if d > dist[u]: continue 
 for v, w in adj[u]: # adj[u] = list of (neighbor, weight) 
 nd = d + w 
 if nd < dist[v]: 
 dist[v] = nd 
 heapq.heappush(h, (nd, v)) 
 return dist 
Time: O((V + E) log V). Does NOT work with negative edges — use Bellman-Ford for that.

you must have come from stair 9 (then +1) OR stair 8 (then +2). So ways(10) = ways(9) + ways(8). 
Notice something? Each sub-answer is used over and over. DP = 'remember once, reuse many 
times.' 
Dynamic Programming solves problems with TWO properties: 
4. Overlapping subproblems — the same smaller problems show up repeatedly. 
5. Optimal substructure — the best answer for the big problem is built from best answers of 
smaller ones.

11.3 The DP 5-step recipe 
6. Define the state: what does dp[i] (or dp[i][j]) mean? 
7. Write the transition: how does dp[i] depend on smaller dp values? 
8. Base cases: the simplest problems you can answer directly. 
9. Iteration order: go small → big so every needed value exists.

11.4 Classic Pattern 1 — 0/1 Knapsack 
You have a bag of capacity W and n items, each with weight w[i] and value v[i]. Maximize the total value 
you can carry. Each item can be taken AT MOST ONCE (that's the '0/1'). 
def knapsack_01(w, v, W): 
 n = len(w) 
 dp = [[0]*(W+1) for _ in range(n+1)] 
 for i in range(1, n+1): 
 for cap in range(W+1): 
 dp[i][cap] = dp[i-1][cap] # skip item i-1 
 if w[i-1] <= cap: # take item i-1 
 dp[i][cap] = max(dp[i][cap], 
 dp[i-1][cap - w[i-1]] + v[i-1]) 
 return dp[n][W] 
 
print(knapsack_01([1,3,4,5], [1,4,5,7], 7)) # 9 
State: dp[i][cap] = best value using the first i items with capacity cap. Time/Space: O(n·W). 
11.5 Unbounded Knapsack 
Same problem but each item can be taken AS MANY times as you like. One-line change: we look at 
dp[i][cap - w] (same row) instead of dp[i-1][cap - w].

 n = len(p) - 1

 j = i + length - 1

 cost = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]

🐍 Section 12 — Greedy Algorithms 
12.1 What is Greedy? 
🐍 The hungry hiker 
A hiker at a fork in the road always takes the path that looks prettiest NOW, without thinking 
about later. Sometimes this lands them at the best destination; sometimes at a cliff. A greedy 
algorithm makes the LOCALLY optimal choice at each step and hopes it leads to the globally best 
solution. 
Greedy works iff the problem has the GREEDY-CHOICE PROPERTY: the locally optimal choice is always 
part of some globally optimal solution. If it isn't, greedy will give a wrong answer and you need DP. 
12.2 When greedy works vs fails 
✅ Works 
Activity Selection (pick earliest finish time), Huffman Encoding, Fractional Knapsack, Dijkstra 
(without negative edges), Prim's/Kruskal's MST, Interval Scheduling. 
🐍 Fails 
0/1 Knapsack (must use DP), Coin Change with weird coin systems (greedy gives wrong answer for 
coins {1,3,4} and amount 6 — picks 4+1+1=3 coins instead of 3+3=2). 
12.3 Activity Selection 
def max_activities(acts): 
 acts.sort(key=lambda x: x[1]) # sort by end time 
 count = 0; end = -1 
 for s, e in acts: 
 if s >= end: 
 count += 1 
 end = e 
 return count 
 
print(max_activities([(1,3),(2,4),(3,5),(0,6),(5,7),(8,9)])) # 4 
Rule: always pick the activity that FINISHES first among the compatible ones. Proof: leaves the most 
room for the rest.

12.4 Fractional Knapsack 
def fractional_knapsack(items, W): 
 items.sort(key=lambda x: -x[1]/x[0]) # value per weight, desc 
 total = 0 
 for w, v in items: 
 if W == 0: break 
 take = min(w, W) 
 total += take * (v / w) 
 W -= take 
 return total 
Here we can take FRACTIONS, so greedy by value/weight ratio is optimal. For 0/1 knapsack it is not. 
12.5 Huffman Encoding (concept) 
Build a prefix-free code where frequent characters get short codes. Algorithm: put all characters in a 
min-heap by frequency; repeatedly pop two smallest and merge them into a new node with combined 
frequency. The tree you build is the code. Used in ZIP, JPEG, MP3.

🐍 Section 13 — Tries (Prefix Trees) 
13.1 What is a Trie? 
🐍 Google autocomplete 
As you type 'pyt' into Google, suggestions like 'python', 'pytorch', 'pythagorean' pop up instantly. 
How? The engine walks a Trie — a tree where each edge is a character and every path from the 
root spells a stored word. 
A Trie (pronounced 'try', from reTRIEval) stores a set of strings so that prefix lookups are O(length of 
prefix), regardless of how many words you stored. 
13.2 Implementation 
class TrieNode: 
 def __init__(self): 
 self.children = {} # char -> TrieNode 
 self.end = False # marks end of a word 
 
class Trie: 
 def __init__(self): 
 self.root = TrieNode() 
 
 def insert(self, word): 
 cur = self.root 
 for c in word: 
 if c not in cur.children: 
 cur.children[c] = TrieNode() 
 cur = cur.children[c] 
 cur.end = True 
 
 def search(self, word): 
 cur = self.root 
 for c in word: 
 if c not in cur.children: return False 
 cur = cur.children[c] 
 return cur.end 
 
 def starts_with(self, prefix): 
 cur = self.root 
 for c in prefix: 
 if c not in cur.children: return False 
 cur = cur.children[c] 
 return True

A HashSet stores full words. To find all words starting with 'pre', you'd iterate ALL words. A Trie

🐍 Section 16 — Advanced Topics (Overview) 
These topics rarely show up in a first-year interview but are golden for advanced rounds and 
competitive programming. We give the shape and use-case of each so you know them when you see 
them. 
16.1 Segment Tree with Lazy Propagation 
Normal segment trees update one index at a time. Lazy propagation lets you UPDATE A WHOLE RANGE 
in O(log n) by 'deferring' the update on internal nodes until you actually need to look at their children. 
Used whenever you need range-updates + range-queries. 
16.2 Sparse Table (Range Minimum Query) 
Pre-compute answers for every interval of length 1, 2, 4, 8, … Any arbitrary range can then be answered 
as the combination of two power-of-2 intervals in O(1). Great when you have many queries but NO 
updates. 
16.3 Heavy-Light Decomposition 
Break a tree into chains so that any path from u to v touches only O(log n) chains. Combined with a 
segment tree on each chain, this answers path queries on trees in O(log² n). 
16.4 KMP and Rabin-Karp (string matching) 
Naive substring search: O(n·m). KMP pre-computes a 'failure function' so it never re-scans — O(n+m). 
Rabin-Karp uses a rolling hash and is very handy for finding many patterns at once. 
# KMP failure function 
def kmp_failure(p): 
 f = [0] * len(p) 
 k = 0 
 for i in range(1, len(p)): 
 while k > 0 and p[k] != p[i]: 
 k = f[k-1] 
 if p[k] == p[i]: 
 k += 1 
 f[i] = k 
 return f

17.5 Common Interview Mistakes (avoid these) 
11. Jumping straight to code without clarifying the problem. Always restate it. 
12. Not asking about edge cases: empty input, one element, negatives, duplicates, overflow. 
13. Ignoring constraints. 'n up to 10^5' means O(n²) might be too slow. 
14. Silent coding. Interviewers can't read minds — narrate. 
15. No dry run. Walk through a small example after writing code — CATCHES BUGS. 
16. Not analyzing complexity. State time AND space. 
17. Forgetting base cases in recursion.

17.6 Problem-Solving Framework (say this out loud) 
21. UNDERSTAND — Repeat the problem. Ask about edge cases, input size, expected output. 
22. EXAMPLE — Run through a small concrete example by hand. 
23. BRUTE FORCE — State the obvious solution and its complexity. 
24. OPTIMIZE — 'Can I avoid repeated work?' Try sorting, hashing, two pointers, BS, DP, etc. 
25. CODE — Write clean code, narrate decisions. 
26. TEST — Run your code on the example mentally. Try edge cases. 
27. COMPLEXITY — State time and space; note trade-offs.
